name: Platform-neutral procurement requirements for AltID and EU standards alignment
consequences: Shifts procurement risk toward platform-neutral fallbacks, reducing vendor lock-in and strengthening resilience. Downstream, it increases negotiation complexity and requires formal interoperability testing to satisfy cross-vendor requirements.
options: Mandate platform-neutral fallback authentication in AltID tender specifications and require demonstrable cross-vendor interoperability as a contract condition.
Embed phased procurement milestones that tie supplier migration to fallback readiness, creating a staged wind-down of dependence on single platform integrity signals.
Pursue formal alignment with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards to ensure Danish requirements leverage broader interoperability and reduce national lock-in.
review: The main trade-off is longer procurement cycles and potential vendor resistance; the three options still may not guarantee timely interoperability certification if EU standards evolve faster than Danish procurement processes.

name: Credible technical demonstrations of platform-neutral fallback
consequences: Validates platform-neutral approaches through credible demonstrators, boosting policy credibility and stakeholder buy-in. Downstream, it concentrates resources on non-production work and must be carefully scoped to avoid scope creep.
options: Build open, standards-based demonstrators using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS to validate cross-vendor authentication flow.
Develop a hardware-token and browser-based fallback prototype to decouple authentication from mobile OS providers and enhance portability.
Arrange independent security reviews and publish results to establish credibility for platform-neutral approaches with external reviewers.
review: Trade-off centers on resource intensity for demonstrators and potential misalignment with live MitID risk posture; the options may not capture long-term scalability or production-readiness constraints.

name: Coalition-building with civil society and policymakers
consequences: Mobilizes political and civil-society support, accelerating procurement and standards discussions. Downstream, it heightens exposure to political cycles and potential shifts in agenda if coalition strength changes.
options: Publish a resilience-focused report and host multi-stakeholder roundtables including civil-society, privacy advocates, policymakers across Denmark.
Engage with Folketinget digital policy committee and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen through formal briefings and written submissions regularly.
Coordinate EU-alignment efforts with European counterparts to influence wallet standards and interoperability policies across the region.
review: Trade-off is political exposure and governance complexity; options may not overcome regulatory inertia or budget constraints that slow coalition-building and EU engagement.

name: Resilience framing through continuity risk and supplier concentration
consequences: Frames resilience as a public-service continuity issue, grounding the case for fallback options in tangible public-service exposure. Downstream, it may trigger broader risk governance requirements that extend beyond the initial fallback focus.
options: Map critical public services and document single-vendor exposure scenarios to justify a platform-neutral fallback architecture.
Publish scenario analyses comparing outage implications under current app-store reliance and fallback alternatives for policymakers.
Develop contingency playbooks with escalation paths, procurement triggers, and fallback activation procedures across public agencies.
review: Trade-off is risk of overstating resilience benefits relative to real operational costs; options may not define objective thresholds for triggering fallback activation.

name: Copenhagen governance and procurement capacity
consequences: Anchors the work in Copenhagen governance, improving legitimacy and access to trusted facilities. Downstream, it risks governance overhead and potential centralization risks; options may not scale beyond Copenhagen without a national expansion plan.
options: Establish a Copenhagen-based governance hub to coordinate stakeholder engagement, procurement drafting, and EU liaison.
Create a rotating consortium of universities and research institutes to staff the demonstration program and ensure redundancy.
Institutionalize secure facilities and access controls to enable sensitive identity research and confidential stakeholder briefings.
review: Trade-off is governance overhead and potential centralization risks; options may not scale beyond Copenhagen without a national expansion plan.

name: EU and international standardization engagement
consequences: Advances EU-wide standardization engagement, aligning Danish requirements with European wallet initiatives. Downstream, it may slow domestic decision-making if EU consensus takes longer than national timelines.
options: Engage with European Digital Identity Wallet working groups to advocate platform-neutral fallback language in formal outputs.
Sponsor joint statements or technical white papers with EU bodies to shape interoperable trust anchors and NSIS-adherent controls.
Assist in drafting procurement guidance aligned with NSIS and eIDAS2 that states platform neutrality as a resilience requirement.
review: Trade-off is alignment risk and slower domestic policy, as EU integration may dilute Danish-specific needs; options may not secure immediate procurement wins.

name: Platform-agnostic identity access standards advocacy
consequences: Adopting platform-agnostic access standards reduces reliance on a single OS as the trust anchor, accelerating resilience through cross-platform interoperability. Downstream, procurement may face longer evaluation cycles and higher initial compliance costs as vendors update architectures to meet new signals, but the long-run risk of lock-in is diminished.
options: Formalize a requirements framework mandating platform-agnostic authentication signals across AltID and future wallets, with explicit fallback pathways.
Lobby for mandatory inclusion of fallback-authentication clauses in all future public-sector identity procurements to reduce vendor lock-in.
Develop a cross-domain standards certification that verifies interoperability across major OS ecosystems and independent trust anchors before supplier eligibility.
review: The primary trade-off is longer procurement cycles and higher early compliance costs, and the three options leave unaddressed the pace at which suppliers can rework architectures, potentially delaying rollout if timelines slip.

name: Open hardware-backed fallback tokens pilot
consequences: Deploying a hardware-backed fallback token path reduces dependence on OS integrity signals by introducing a platform-neutral root of trust. Downstream, the program requires secure token provisioning, revocation, and user onboarding workflows; it may also raise cost and training needs and face hardware supply constraints.
options: Prototype a hardware-token backed fallback path using open standards such as FIDO2, tested in a Denmark-based pilot with public-sector participants.
Establish a browser-based fallback authentication path leveraging portable credentials across devices, independent of OS vendors.
Create a governance pilot to evaluate the interoperability of a cloud-hosted, platform-neutral identity broker usable by multiple agencies.
review: The leverage here prioritizes hardware-based trust, but the options introduce supply-chain, user onboarding, and cost risks that may slow adoption if hardware procurement cannot scale quickly.

name: Strategic reserve of cross-platform trust anchors
consequences: Creating a governance-owned reserve of platform-neutral trust anchors reduces single-point failure risk and provides resilience during vendor transitions. Downstream, it requires ongoing governance, secure custody, and funding commitments to maintain readiness.
options: Establish a legally protected stockpile of certified fallback tokens and governance-owned trust anchors for surge continuity.
Partner with national labs to maintain independent cryptographic key material in secure platform-neutral environments.
Develop a phased funding mechanism to stockpile fallback credentials aligned with NSIS guidance and procurement deadlines.
review: The stockpile concept reduces single-point failure risk, yet the three options expose governance complexity and maintenance costs that could erode value if oversight is weak or access rules lag.

name: Legal and regulatory risk framing for fallback identity
consequences: Framing regulatory risk clarifies the cost of dependency and builds legitimacy for platform-neutral paths. Downstream, it may trigger political attention but could delay procurement if debates stall implementation or enforcement is uneven.
options: Draft a regulatory impact assessment mapping platform dependence risks to continuity-of-service scenarios for critical services.
Initiate a parliamentary briefing to secure digital sovereignty language within procurement policy and AltID-related legislative work.
Publish a risk register with scenario analyses highlighting regulatory gaps to catalyze policy reform discussions.
review: The trade-off centers on regulatory time and political capital; the options push for reform readiness, but gaps in enforcement and inter-agency alignment may render reforms aspirational rather than practical.

name: EU-aligned interoperability and procurement pressure harmonization
consequences: Coordinating with EU standards accelerates alignment and leverages cross-border momentum. Downstream, national tailoring and timeline alignment risks create potential misfits with domestic procurement cycles and sovereignty concerns.
options: Lead an EU-focused interoperability consortium to align AltID with EUDI Wallet requirements and timelines across public sector deployments.
Coordinate with NSIS and the European Commission to secure a formal channel for Danish fallback requirements in cross-border use.
Publish a position paper advocating for platform-neutral fallback criteria in EU-level procurement framework tender templates.
review: EU-level pressure can accelerate alignment, but the options leave unaddressed potential sovereignty concerns and domestic procurement constraints that could complicate rapid national implementation if EU timelines diverge.

name: Public-sector capability development for platform-neutral procurement oversight
consequences: Building internal capability reduces exposure to vendor lock-in by equipping agencies with neutrality criteria. Downstream, it demands sustained leadership, budget, and governance discipline to avoid drift and ensure consistent evaluation.
options: Create a cross-agency task force to audit platform-dependence risks and track procurement outcomes consistently across agencies.
Implement a training module for procurement officers on evaluating fallback authentication and openness criteria rigorously.
Launch a transparent stakeholder scoreboard that tracks neutrality commitments, continuity milestones, and vendor-diversification progress over time.
review: The internal capability lever reduces exposure, yet the options require sustained leadership and budget; if training and governance bodies rotate, momentum may falter and neutrality commitments slip.

name: Platform-neutral fallback integration into AltID procurement contracts
consequences: Imposing platform-neutral fallback requirements tightens procurement obligations on suppliers and creates a formal path for interoperability. Downstream, this can extend procurement cycles and raise compliance costs while creating clearer expectations for vendors, potentially excluding non-conforming bids.
options: Incorporate explicit platform-neutral fallback authentication requirements into AltID procurement contracts with measurable milestones.
Mandate interoperability clauses in procurement that require vendor-neutral verification of identity signals across ecosystems.
Institute a phased evaluation process that awards pilots for fallback demonstrations in procurement before wider deployment.
review: Primary trade-off centers on extended procurement timelines and higher vendor compliance costs, while the three options still leave ambiguity about how to enforce platform neutrality across existing supplier contracts and how to measure real interoperability.

name: Continuity architecture trials across multi-ecosystem environments
consequences: Rolling trials anchor continuity planning through cross-ecosystem testing, exposing gaps between platform signals and fallback paths. Downstream, governance overhead grows and duplication risk arises if multiple services run parallel pilots without centralized coordination.
options: Establish a national continuity exercise that runs scheduled fallback authentication in simulated outages across several public services.
Develop a cross-ecosystem prototype environment with Windows, Linux, and Android hardware tokens to test fallback viability.
Create a governance mechanism to coordinate between Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID vendors for rapid decision-making during outages.
review: The main trade-off is increased governance overhead and potential duplication of effort, while the options do not resolve how funding will be sustained beyond phase boundaries or ensure cross-agency coordination.

name: Credible open-standards demonstrator program
consequences: Two non-production demonstrators will provide evidence of feasibility and influence policy language; successes can shift procurement expectations. Downstream, there is a risk of scope creep and non-production results not translating into live-system changes.
options: Commission two non-production demonstrators using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on privacy-respecting platforms to show interoperability.
Publish independent security review reports of demonstrators to build trust with policymakers.
Coordinate with EU working groups to align demonstrations with European Digital Identity Wallet guidance.
review: Trade-off lies in non-production demonstrations risking limited policy impact; the options do not guarantee a direct pathway from demonstrators to formal procurement changes.

name: Strategic coalition mandates for procurement neutrality and resilience
consequences: Formal coalitions can build political legitimacy and reduce single-vendor dependence, but may slow decision-making if stakeholder agendas diverge. Downstream, progress depends on maintaining a coherent governance framework and timely decision rights.
options: Formalize a multi-stakeholder charter that binds public agencies to prioritize platform-neutral resilience procurement.
Organize a public-audience accountable report-back mechanism with parliamentary committees to track progress.
Establish a rapid-response advisory board including privacy advocates and security researchers to influence legislation.
review: The main trade-off is risk of coalition capture and slow decision-making; the options lack a concrete escalation path if political attention shifts away from the issue.

name: EU-aligned interoperability pressure through standards engagement
consequences: Aligning with EU standards reduces cross-border lock-in and supports harmonized procurement language. Downstream, national prerogatives may be constrained if EU requirements take precedence and domestic implementation timelines may slow.
options: Seek formal alignment with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards through joint statements and technical surveys.
Pilot cross-border fallback demonstrations with selected EU partners to demonstrate portability.
Institute a regulatory impact assessment to map Danish fallback needs against EU requirements.
review: The trade-off is potential friction with national procurement sovereignty as EU alignment pressures grow; the options do not resolve how to reconcile Danish timelines with EU standards.

name: Resilience-focused public communications and governance transparency
consequences: A resilience framing reduces political risk and improves public trust by explaining risk scenarios and mitigations. Downstream, messaging may outpace technical reality or constrain policy options if overextended, and it could invite heightened scrutiny of procurement choices.
options: Launch a resilience-focused communications campaign demonstrating risk scenarios and mitigations.
Publish an open decision-log and governance rubrics to preserve transparency in procurement choices.
Host open workshops with civil society to collect feedback and build trust with the public.
review: The trade-off is potential overemphasis on messaging at the expense of tangible milestones; the options do not specify quantitative metrics to translate resilience claims into policy decisions.
